does: This paper presents a systematic empirical evaluation of automated prompt injection attacks against LLM agents using the AgentDojo framework. The authors adapt two automated attack methods—gradient-based GCG and black-box TAP—to work against agents that interact with untrusted external data. They evaluate these attacks across 80 task pairs spanning four domains and multiple models, measuring attack success rates and transfer properties. Key findings show that black-box optimization substantially outperforms gradient-based methods due to GCG's optimization instability under realistic compute constraints. The study reveals that TAP's effectiveness varies with the attacker model itself, where stronger models generate more effective injections while safety-tuned models tend to refuse generating adversarial content. Task-universal attacks demonstrate strong transfer to unseen tasks and out-of-distribution domains.
connects: This work builds on the extensive literature on adversarial attacks against large language models, particularly jailbreaking and prompt injection research. While automated attack methods like GCG and TAP have proven effective for jailbreaking standalone models, their application to agentic systems remains underexplored. The paper extends this line of work by studying indirect prompt injection—where attacks come through untrusted external data sources rather than direct user input. It connects to research on agent vulnerability and trustworthiness, as well as studies examining how model capabilities and safety training affect adversarial robustness. The work also relates to transfer attack literature by examining how attacks generalize across tasks and models.
enables: This work establishes baseline measurements and methodologies for understanding prompt injection vulnerabilities in deployed LLM agents, which can guide future defensive research and safety-critical system design. The finding that black-box methods outperform gradient-based approaches suggests that gradient-based defenses may be less relevant for agent security. The demonstrated effectiveness of task-universal attacks highlights the need for robust, generalizable defenses rather than task-specific mitigations. Results about safety training's mixed effects on attack generation point toward future work on better aligning model behavior to refuse harmful outputs while maintaining utility. The comprehensive evaluation framework and findings enable the development of more secure agent architectures and defense mechanisms tailored to realistic threat models where agents consume untrusted external data.
